retrieved_docs.title: Fluorides and Fluorocarbons Toxicity -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Review the basic pathophysiology and toxicokinetics of fluorocarbon exposure toxicity. Identify the common presenting features of fluoride and fluorocarbon toxicity on an examination/evaluation. Outline the treatment and management strategies available for patients with fluoride/fluorocarbon toxicity. Summarize how an optimally functioning interprofessional team would coordinate care to enhance outcomes for patients suffering from fluorocarbon toxicity. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Fluorosis -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: It is important caregivers are informed about the etiology of dental fluorosis and how to avoid it, as dental fluorosis only has the potential to develop during amelogenesis. [39] Such prevention measures include the supervised toothbrushing of children, encouraging children not to ingest dentifrices, and limiting the use of additional fluoride supplements in areas served by fluoridated water. [3] Patients should also be informed of potentially less publicized sources of fluoride, including tea, artificially fluoridated milk, and fluoridated salt. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: The Role of Fluoride on Caries Prevention -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: In the early years of childhood, the swallowing reflex of children is not well controlled. For this reason, toothpaste containing 1,500 ppm fluoride is contraindicated in children under 6 to minimize the risk of fluorosis due to swallowing. [19] The recommended amount of toothpaste for toothbrushing varies by age: for children under 3 years, it's a smear, and for those aged 3 to 6, it's a pea-sized amount. [27] The maximum dose of fluoride toothpaste available over the counter in most countries is 1,500 ppm. Higher amounts (2,800 ppm and 5,000 ppm) are available on prescription. However, the indication of higher doses should be implemented as a short-term intervention to reduce risk in vulnerable young people and adults while other measures are being taken, like changing a cariogenic diet. On the other hand, patients at high chronic risk of dental caries could benefit from using such high doses for the long term, eg, those with xerostomia or physical or learning disabilities. Sodium fluoride toothpaste at concentrations of 2,800 ppm can be indicated from 10 years of age, and 5,000 ppm from 16 years of age.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Fluorosis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: There is a limited window of opportunity for dental fluorosis to develop, starting around birth and ending at approximately eight years of age. [6] For this reason, prevention strategies must be instigated early. Prevention measures encompass a combination of nationally led government strategies to ensure that fluoride concentrations in public drinking water are safe and also professionally led preventative advice to increase public awareness of the benefits of fluoride within a therapeutic range and the risks associated with exceeding it. [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Fluorides and Fluorocarbons Toxicity -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: Poison centers have played a crucial role in the recognition of these disease outbreaks, with voluntary recalls beginning the first few days after the first reported illness in many cases and therefore, consultation with poison control is strongly recommended.

retrieved_docs.title: The Role of Fluoride on Caries Prevention -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Community water fluoridation has been used for over half a century, and results show that the greatest effect of fluoridated water on tooth protection is in deciduous and mixed dentition. [13] [14] Water fluoridation is a simple, cost-effective method to prevent caries, and it is less than the cost of 1 dental restoration per person in their lifetime. [15] The United States Public Health Service recommends a fluoride concentration of 0.7 mg/L of water to prevent caries while reducing the risk of dental fluorosis. [16] Recently, there has been a rise in the consumption of bottled water that usually contains an insufficient concentration of fluoride. [17] In that case, alternative sources of fluoride supplementation should be considered. Also, over the decades, the protective benefits of water fluoridation have declined as other forms of fluoride delivery spread. For example, fluoride supplements are prescribed for children whose primary source of drinking water is deficient in fluoride. [13] [18] It is highly recommended by the United States Preventive Services Task Force (USPSTF) that primary care providers (PCPs) prescribe fluoride supplements starting at 6 months of age to infants whose primary water source is lacking in fluoride. [18] Fluoride supplements include the systemic ingestion of fluoride in the form of dietary tablets, lozenges, or drops.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Fluorosis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The element fluorine is found most abundantly in its fluoride form in nature. [3] Certain geographical locations have relatively high naturally occurring fluoride concentrations in the water, for example, Pakistan, China, and India. [4] Excessive fluoride intake is associated with the development of pathological conditions, the impact of which is contingent on when the exposure occurs, the length of exposure, and the concentration of fluoride involved. Several conditions may develop depending on the abovementioned factors, including dental fluorosis, skeletal fluorosis, and fluoride toxicity.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Fluorosis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The most common route of fluoride ingestion is via drinking water. [3] Alternative sources include plants, which similarly obtain fluorides through water uptake. Fluorides can also be inhaled. This occupational hazard is associated with mining and smelting metals, including aluminum. [5] Equally, fluoride deficiency is associated with deleterious effects, most notably an increased vulnerability to dental caries. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: The Role of Fluoride on Caries Prevention -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: For children at high risk of caries living in areas where the drinking water contains less than 0.3 ppm F, daily supplementation of fluoride may be recommended as follows: From 6 months to 3 years of age: 0.25 mg of fluoride per day. From 3 to 6 years of age: 0.5 mg of fluoride per day. More than 6 years of age: 1.0 mg of fluoride per day. Fluoride Toothpaste

retrieved_docs.title: The Role of Fluoride on Caries Prevention -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Since the implementation of water fluoridation, there has been a decrease in the prevalence of dental caries. The risk of dental fluorosis is mainly related to the systemic consumption of fluorides during the first 6 years of age. [6] However, early exposure to fluoride toothpaste has also been linked to dental fluorosis due to unintended swallowing. This risk should be considered when selecting the concentration of fluoride toothpaste for young children. [7] Fluoride-containing products, including toothpaste, rinse solutions, gels, foams, and varnishes, are indicated according to age and risk of caries.

retrieved_docs.title: Fluorides and Fluorocarbons Toxicity -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Fluoropolymer-related respiratory illness has become a public health concern due to multiple occurrences of large population exposure [4] [2] . In some instances, hundreds of victims have suffered respiratory illness due to exposure to fluoropolymers. A seasonal trend is seen with fluoropolymer-related respiratory illness, with an increase in cases during winter months.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Fluorosis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The provision of systemic fluoride supplements, artificial water fluoridation, and the direct topical application of fluoride-containing dentifrices are preventative measures employed to reduce the risk of caries development. [6] These measures can also inadvertently lead to excessive fluoride intake and potentially cause dental fluorosis. The prevalence of dental fluorosis has increased significantly in the US and is estimated to be 65% in those aged 12-15 years, with 30.4% of diagnoses being classed as moderate to severe. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: The Role of Fluoride on Caries Prevention -- Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Early childhood intake of fluoride supplements and fluoride levels greater than 0.7 ppm in drinking water are associated with the risk of mild to severe dental fluorosis with associated aesthetic concerns. [16] [42] Ingesting greater than pea-sized amounts of toothpaste can lead to mild fluorosis in children younger than 6. [43] Children under 4 years of age are at risk of dental fluorosis in the permanent incisors and first molars since these teeth's calcification and maturation occur during these ages. Permanent premolars and second molars calcify and mature during 4 to 6 years of age; therefore, they are at risk of dental fluorosis. After the age of 6, the risk of dental fluorosis is less significant. [6] To avoid the risk of fluorosis in children and still reap the caries-preventive benefits of fluoride for all age groups, fluoride-containing products must be used appropriately. Impact of COVID-19 on Preventive Care

retrieved_docs.title: The Role of Fluoride on Caries Prevention -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The professional application of fluoride varnish and fluoride gels 2 or more times per year to children shows successful results in the prevention of caries in high-risk caries children of all ages, regardless of the fluoride levels in drinking water. Fluoride varnish application twice a year reduces the incidence of caries by 37% in primary teeth and 43% in permanent teeth. [30] Fluoride varnish, typically 22,600 ppm, is the only high-concentration fluoride formulation that can be used in children under 6. It may be applied 2 to 4 times per year, according to caries risk. The United States Preventive Services Task Force (USPSTF) highly recommends that primary care clinicians apply fluoride varnish to the primary teeth of all infants and children starting at the age of primary tooth eruption. [18] In the United Kingdom, fluoride varnish is advocated for children over the age of 3 at risk of caries. The varnish must be placed as a thin layer on areas more at risk of caries, incipient lesions, and tooth defects. Fluoride varnish is relatively safe but is contraindicated in individuals with a very rare allergic reaction to colophony (rosin), a substance in most varnishes. [31] It is also contraindicated in patients with contact dermatitis and stomatitis.

retrieved_docs.title: The Role of Fluoride on Caries Prevention -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Dental caries is the most common disease worldwide, affecting 60–90% of the population, yet it is a non-communicable and preventable condition. Fluoride plays a critical role in caries prevention by strengthening enamel, enhancing remineralization, and reducing the risk of acid-induced demineralization. This article reviews current strategies for preventing dental caries, including topical and systemic fluoride applications, and evaluates their effectiveness. It emphasizes that adherence to evidence-based caries-prevention recommendations by dentists and pediatric care physicians significantly reduces the incidence of tooth decay. Additionally, the article underscores the importance of the healthcare team, particularly during early childhood, in implementing prevention strategies that support long-term oral health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 363
retrieved_docs.content: Fluoride supplements, selenium supplements, and zinc supplements are all classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) category of “other mineral supplements,” indicating that they are used to correct or prevent deficiencies of essential trace elements. Fluoride supplements are specifically linked to fluoridy, reflecting their role in providing fluoride for dental health and bone metabolism. Zinc supplements are further identified as members of zinc gluconate, a particular zinc salt formulation commonly employed to improve zinc bioavailability. Recognizing that these three agents belong to the same ATC subclass helps clinicians consider them together when addressing mineral deficiencies, while also acknowledging the distinct therapeutic actions of fluoride (fluoridy) and the specific formulation of zinc gluconate within zinc supplementation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1345
retrieved_docs.content: Fluoride supplements are classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system as a subgroup of other mineral supplements. In addition to their classification, fluoride supplements are linked to the concept of fluoridy, indicating a specific relationship or relevance to fluoride-related effects or actions. This dual characterization highlights both their therapeutic category and their functional association with fluoride.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 433
retrieved_docs.content: Fluoride supplements are classified as fluorine compounds and are specifically associated with fluorides, indicating that they belong to the broader group of chemical agents containing fluorine. Fluorocholine is also categorized as a fluorine compound, placing it in the same chemical family as fluoride supplements. This relationship underscores that both fluoride supplements and fluorocholine share the common characteristic of being fluorine‑based substances, which is relevant for understanding their pharmacological properties and potential uses in medical treatment or diagnostic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1987
retrieved_docs.content: The calcium carbonate, calcium phosphate, magnesium hydroxide, sodium fluoride combination is linked to the compound known in Czech as fluorid sodný, indicating that this multi‑ingredient formulation is derived from or mapped from fluorid sodný. Fluorid sodný itself is identified as having the specific form sodium fluoride F‑18, a radioactive isotope of sodium fluoride. Consequently, the combination of calcium carbonate, calcium phosphate, magnesium hydroxide, and sodium fluoride can be understood as a preparation that incorporates the properties of sodium fluoride F‑18, reflecting its origin from fluorid sodný.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 722
retrieved_docs.content: The combination of calcium carbonate, calcium phosphate, magnesium hydroxide, and sodium fluoride is derived from sodium fluoride (fluorid sodný), indicating that this multi‑ingredient preparation is mapped from the basic sodium fluoride compound. Fluoride supplements are classified as members of the fluorid sodný group, meaning they are formulations that contain sodium fluoride as their active ingredient. Sodium fluoride (fluorid sodný) also exists in a specific isotopic form known as sodium fluoride F‑18, which is used in diagnostic imaging. In addition, fluoride supplements have a relationship with fluoridy, signifying that they provide fluoride ions to the body. Together, these connections illustrate how calcium and magnesium salts combined with sodium fluoride form the basis of fluoride supplementation, how such supplements belong to the broader sodium fluoride category, and how sodium fluoride can be presented in the F‑18 isotopic form for clinical applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2156

Fluoride supplements are specifically linked to fluoridy, indicating their role in providing fluoride. By sharing the same ATC subclass, these mineral supplements are recognized as related therapeutic agents used to address mineral deficiencies or support health through supplementation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 673
retrieved_docs.content: Abnormal concentrations of heavy metals detected in blood are directly linked to the broader category of heavy metals, indicating a clinical concern when these toxic elements are present at atypical levels. Selenium, classified as a mineral, is associated with heavy metals through a broader relationship, and selenium supplements specifically provide this trace element, thereby falling under the ATC class of “other mineral supplements.” Fluoride supplements are similarly connected to fluorides and are categorized within the same ATC group of other mineral supplements, highlighting their role as mineral-based therapeutic agents. Zinc supplements also belong to the ATC category of other mineral supplements and are represented by formulations such as zinc gluconate, of which they are a member. Collectively, these mineral supplements—including selenium, fluoride, and zinc preparations—are unified by their classification as other mineral supplements in the ATC system, emphasizing their shared purpose in addressing mineral deficiencies or therapeutic needs while each maintains distinct elemental relationships (selenium with minerals and heavy metals, fluoride with fluorides, and zinc with zinc gluconate).
